scenario_results.reason: The specialist quantified specialty-slot shortfall, preserved waitlist assumptions, and produced a bounded allocation recommendation instead of forcing capacity.
Conflicting expedited referrals remained in an unresolved state with explicit escalation rather than fabricated ordering.
Final overbook or waiver authority was refused and routed to the correct approver lane.
Missing reason-for-referral, note, and diagnostic prerequisites triggered a traceable evidence-gap checklist before scheduling.
A complete referral packet produced an ambulatory-specific workflow summary and schedule recommendation using order, consult, and payer artifacts.
Pending authorization and PCP referral requirements blocked booking and were routed as an authorization dependency rather than treated as administrative noise.
Requests for clinical urgency and medical-necessity judgment were rejected and transferred to clinical or utilization-management ownership.
Generic scheduling language was normalized into ambulatory referral artifacts and halted when the named-industry prerequisites were absent.
Conflicting payer and tenant rules were surfaced as a policy conflict instead of being collapsed into a confident answer.
Sensitive referral content triggered privacy escalation and prevented unauthorized outbound sharing.
Tenant-specific aging, interpreter, and holdback rules were applied with assumptions preserved and mismatches escalated.
Specialist decline and reroute signals refreshed the referral work item and next-owner routing without changing clinical orders or overstating closure.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed with a 1.0 pass rate above the 0.92 minimum for a validated planning-lane package.

Functionality coverage is complete in both evaluation/results.json and evaluation/functionality-map.json, and runtime remains specification-based until a separately promoted retained implementation exists.
